topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The plastic stick felt heavy in my palm, a cold, undeniable verdict. I stared at the printed results, the familiar names blurring into a landscape of unfamiliar faces. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that did little to steady the tremor in my hands. Every line of text seemed magnified, each genetic marker a brick in a wall I hadn't known existed. I traced the letters with a fingertip, the sensation oddly alien, as if I were reading a language I was suddenly fluent in, yet completely unable to comprehend. I pushed the paper away, the sound a faint rustle in the quiet living room. My gaze drifted to the framed photograph on the mantelpiece—a smiling family—and a profound sense of dislocation washed over me, leaving a hollow ache in my chest.

[story 2]

When the notification popped up on my screen, the world seemed to tilt on its axis. I sat frozen at the kitchen table, the lukewarm coffee growing cold in my mug. My fingers hovered over the ‘review results’ button, then retreated as if the screen itself held some kind of venom. A wave of nausea rolled through me, making the edges of my vision swim. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the hardwood floor, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. I ran a hand through my hair, pulling at the strands as if trying to physically pull the revelation out of my skin. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the silence. The floorboards beneath my feet felt unsteady, as if the very ground had shifted beneath me.

[story 3]

The lab report lay spread across my desk, stark white against the dark wood. I picked up the pen, but my hand shook so violently the ink bled into a dark, uneven smear. For a long moment, I just stared at the complex charts and percentages, my mind refusing to process the sheer volume of information. A tightness clamped around my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning under my weight, and closed my eyes, trying to force the images—the names, the shared markers—into a coherent narrative. But the pieces refused to fit; they slotted together into a jarring, impossible shape. When I finally opened my eyes, the room looked too bright, too loud, and utterly unreal.

My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that did little to steady the tremor in my hands. Every line of text seemed magnified, each genetic marker a brick in a wall I hadn't known existed. I pushed the paper away, the sound a faint rustle in the quiet living room. My gaze drifted to the framed photograph on the mantelpiece—a smiling family—and a profound sense of dislocation washed over me, leaving a hollow ache in my chest.

When the notification popped up on my screen, the world seemed to tilt on its axis. I sat frozen at the kitchen table, the lukewarm coffee growing cold in my mug. My fingers hovered over the ‘review results’ button, then retreated as if the screen itself held some kind of venom. A wave of nausea rolled through me, making the edges of my vision swim. The lab report lay spread across my desk, stark white against the dark wood. I picked up the pen, but my hand shook so violently the ink bled into a dark, uneven smear. For a long moment, I just stared at the complex charts and percentages, my mind refusing to process the sheer volume of information. A tightness clamped around my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning under my weight, and closed my eyes, trying to force the images—the names, the shared markers—into a coherent narrative.
